data.title: Politics And The Markets 08/01/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Progressive: No Longer A Premium On This Insurer
data.description:
At the end of September of last year, I labeled Progressive (NYSE:PGR) a 'buy' in my article on this insurer. And Progressive definitely delivered operationally so far; however, I also mentioned that the valuations at the time were somewhat stretched. The market agreed, and even though the company performed really well, the current share price is lower than in September. This multiple compression has further improved Progressive, making it a strong buy at current levels.

Performance overview

Looking at the financial performance over the period since my previous rating, it is clear that there is a big underperformance as compared to the S&P 500:

To be clear, less than a year is a very limited window to judge performance, but the gap to a broad benchmark such as the S&P 500 is significant. Short-term shareholders are most likely not very happy with such a run. Do note the roughly 2% difference between price return and total return is due to the 10 cents quarterly dividend and the special distribution of USD 4.50 earlier this year.

I already mentioned that the return development can largely be ascribed to compression of multiples for the business. The table below is proof of it.

Previously the company had a TTM P/E of 22.4, and now it stands at a more reasonable ~ 14. This is far below the 5Y average for Progressive, so by this metric along, the current share price is far more appealing. Without covering each single metric listed, the overall picture is that on practically every valuation metric the business is now cheaper than it was last year. And also largely cheaper than Progressive traded on average over the last five years.

While the share price contracted, the underlying business gave no reason for this contraction. If we compare the amount of policies in force at the end of August 2024 compared to the end of June 2025, we can see that this has grown by over 10%, to a total number of more than 37.3 million active policies.

For a mature company such as Progressive, this is an impressive amount of growth in less than a year.

Growth in the number of policies is not the only great growth number. Looking at the table below, many growth metrics are still far beyond the sector median.

In many cases the difference is several hundred percent better. And again often better than the 5Y average of Progressive itself. EPS figures in this regard are a bit more wonky, since this is significantly impacted by movements of the financial markets. These movements can cause accounting losses or gains of the sizable investment portfolio of Progressive, which largely consists of bonds. Taking this into account, the overall growth profile does look excellent.

Profitability is another area that displays great strengths. In my previous article I wrote the following:

'In this sector, Return on Equity is one of my preferred yardsticks to evaluate performance and with a 5-year average of over 22%, Progressive performance far exceeds the sector median of 10.33%. When looking at Return on Assets, the comparison becomes even better. Lastly, seeing how little capex is needed from total revenue to run the business, is great. In general, you want businesses that require little capex to operate, but can still absorb large amounts of capital, which can be used without diminished rates of return. Progressive is such a business and this is one of the prime reasons why the performance over the decades has been so good as it is.'

I still stand by these words, and it is actually quite amazing to see that a decades old car insurance business of this scale is able to keep on delivering and improving. Return on Equity now even stands at over 37%. You would expect such numbers for a high-flying tech company, but not for Progressive. Admittedly, Progressive of course uses its fair share of refined technology to achieve these numbers, but still, it is remarkable.

Having looked at both the performance of Progressive, the share and of Progressive, the business, it to me now seems that it is an exceptionally well-run insurance company, and has been for decades and now trades as an ordinary company that does not have such a track record.

Upcoming earnings

Next week Progressive will release its full earnings report for Q2 and its investor relations department will host an event to amongst other things discuss the pricing mechanics. While all details of the Q2 performance will only be known next week, much is already known about it since Progressive very handily publishes monthly reports. So let's inspect what is available:

Diluted comprehensive income for H1 2025 clocks in at USD 12.03 per share versus USD 6.25 in the same period last year. This is an actual improvement of over 90%. Again, not bad at all. This increase can be attributed to several factors. One is the increase in policies in force, as previously mentioned.

The second factor stems from the strong combined ratio, which stands at 86.2 for Q2 2025 as compared to 91.9 for Q2 2024.

For those who are not familiar with the term combined ratio, it states in essence how profitable an insurance company is on its underwriting operations. The number translates to the amount of costs incurred for net premiums written. If the number is above hundred, an insurance loses money on its underwriting operations. In general it can be said that lower is better.

The final factor that helped improve the comprehensive income for the period is the increase of pretax annualized investment income book yield. Which rose to 4.2% from 3.8% at the end of H1 2024. As said, Progressive has like many insurance companies a sizable portfolio of investments and this is now also yielding higher. With the Fed not yet lowering rates, I expect this area of the business to keep performing in similar fashion for the foreseeable future. This combination of growing sales, favourable combined ratios, and a higher yielding portfolio is an excellent engine to drive future growth.

Risks

Previously I mentioned multiple contraction as a risk factor, and that seems to have been right in hindsight so far. While I deem the current multiples very attractive, this does not mean that this could go down further. After all, Progressive is still trading at a premium to many other insurance companies. The risk regarding loosening underwriting standards also something that needs to be monitored. Given the fine results in this regard it does not seem to be an issue so far, but once the combined ratio for a longer period starts creeping up, it does become a red flag. Other side of this coin is that competitors could be willing to underwrite insurances at a poorer standard. This for example to seize market share. While this would directly impact Progressive's combined ratio, it could hurt its sales growth.

Conclusion

Previously I called Progressive a 'buy' and so far that has been a mixed bag. Operationally this was absolutely correct. But also my risk warning of multiple contraction seems to have been the right call so far. The result is that as compared with September, that Progressive as an investment has seriously underperformed. However, I am now even stronger convinced by Progressive as an investment. Not only is it now more likely that operational performance will deliver satisfying results, no, now it is also a less risky proposition due to the more reasonable multiple to be paid per share. This does not just reduce downside risk, but offers potential multiple expansion if Progressive starts trading inline with its 5Y average multiples. All these elements combined make Progressive a 'strong buy'.

data.title: AIA: Not The Best Time To Make Concentrated Bets On Larger Asian Markets
data.description:
Time to Reconsider Dominant Large Caps in Major Asian Markets

Asian equities have substantially outperformed emerging markets in the long run, and many markets appear better positioned in terms of valuation and growth potential.

The iShares Asia 50 ETF (NASDAQ:AIA) has outperformed the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (EEM) by over 40 percentage points in the past decade.

As an investment vehicle, this ETF has been very effective at tracking its benchmark index, which consists of the top 50 largest equities in Asia. It has also delivered stable long-term returns.

While Asian equities have outperformed substantially, there are still many issues that make investing in some of the larger markets riskier for H2 2025.

  • ETFs like this have very concentrated exposure to China and Taiwan, a move that carries more risk based on political events in recent weeks.
  • Dominant Asian markets trade at a premium to MSCI Emerging Markets, after having a strong run in H1 2025. Meanwhile, emerging Asian markets trade at much more compelling valuation.
  • Asian equities have performed very well so far this year, despite trade war and macro concerns, but this has left valuations stretched in many of these markets
  • Investing in these markets also leaves you heavily exposed to IT and financial stocks, with minimal exposure to other favorable themes like consumer staples.

Based on recent movements in the equity markets and macro/political developments, it makes sense to be underweight China and Taiwan, especially since these markets make up nearly half of the MSCI EM Index and nearly three fourths of this ETF.

This article will cover some of the macro developments in these markets, coupled with favorable equity market movements in H1 2025, and explore some of the more undiscovered Asian markets.

Overview

There are several important characteristics to note about this ETF, which make it better to seek other investment vehicles.

The management fee of 0.5% is relatively high, especially since it merely tracks dominant larger caps in Asian markets, but still slightly lower than that of some peer ETFs like the MSCI Emerging Markets ETF and the Global X FTSE Southeast Asia ETF (ASEA).

Although it invests in over 50 listed companies, it is strongly dominated by its top 10 holdings. Moreover, this ETF trades at a slight premium to MSCI EMs, and there are plenty of undervalued Asian alternatives to consider.

The index this ETF tracks invests in 50 of the largest companies in Asia, found in markets like China, Taiwan, Hong Kong, Singapore, and South Korea. The average market cap is between $2.7-$941 billion, and the median market cap is $36.3 billion.

The first and most obvious issue to consider is the dominance of the top companies in this ETF's weighting. Taiwan Semiconductor accounts for 23% of this ETF's assets, and the top 5 companies account for nearly half of this ETF's assets.

While it invests in a variety of other smaller companies, most accounting for less than 3% of the ETF's assets, these names are all heavily concentrated in tech and banking stocks in China and Taiwan. Around 74% of its assets are invested in China and Taiwan, and its IT and financial holdings account for around 64% of its total assets.

Dominant themes like energy, real estate, healthcare, and industrials only account for less than 4% of its total assets, while markets like Hong Kong and Singapore are not significant contributors to its performance.

Growth Outlook with a Focus on China

The main concern on many investors' minds has been Q2 2025 growth, yet this outlook appears very favorable in many markets. The US economy shocked economists by delivering 3% YoY growth last quarter, easily dodging a recession.

Many economies in Asia have also had strong economic performance.

China's Economy Largely Undisturbed: China's economy also has a relatively favorable growth outlook, despite trade war tensions. China's economy grew by over 5% during the first half of this year, driven by rising industrial output, exports, and increased investments. Retail sales in China also rose by 5%.

One important fact to note is that China's export growth has been very healthy, as it has been able to diversify away from the United States to other partners.

China's manufacturing PMI rose to over 50 last month, a substantial recovery from May.

Taiwan Economy Outlook: Latest estimates for Taiwan show that the country's economic growth in Q2 2025 likely topped growth in Q1 2025.

Taiwan's export growth has been very healthy during the first half of 2025, despite concerns over the trade war. The greater risk in Taiwan lies in the general cyclical nature of the semiconductor industry, as seen in years like 2023.

South Korea Economy: South Korea has been a slight area of concern, as political risks have soared and concerns over electronics export-oriented growth have also risen. Korea's Central Bank projects that growth will fall below 1% this year, driven by weaker exports and slower consumption growth. South Korean equities have slowly begun to catch up with some Asian peers, after rebounding substantially after Q1 2025.

Asia Outperforms Global Equities: 2025 has been a healthy year for Asian equities so far. Asia Ex. Japan markets have continued to outperform in 2025, ahead of the S&P 500 and European markets.

China has been one of the main drivers of this solid performance in Asian markets.

As we have seen during previous trade war tensions during the previous Trump administration, major Asian equity markets still have the potential to perform well amid trade war tensions. However, a 20+% run in markets like China seems a bit overdone, and these markets could be due for a pullback on the back of any unexpected economic news.

Looking Elsewhere

The lack of geographical and industry diversification seems to be a common issue among Asia-focused ETFs. The Global X FTSE Southeast Asia also invests over 60% of its assets in 2 markets, and invests around 60% of its assets in financials.

Equity markets like the Philippines, Malaysia, Indonesia, and Thailand trade at around 11-14x earnings, a significant discount to this ETF.

These markets have been shielded from some of the dominant Asia large-cap market gains, and have consequently underperformed this ETF by 24-40 percentage points. However, these ETFs are much more undervalued at the moment and have a much more diversified structure.

ETF Top 3 Industries Data ( % of total assets) Top 10 Holdings % of Total Assets
iShares MSCI Thailand (THD)

Industrials: 23.3%

Energy: 16.0%

Financials: 11.5%

55.5%
VanEck Indonesia ETF (IDX)

Financials: 24.9%

Basic Materials: 23.7%

Energy: 11.5%

56.8%
iShares MSCI Philippines ETF (EPHE)

Financials: 23.2%

Industrials: 22.8%

Consumer Cyclical: 17.8%

64.6%
iShares MSCI Malaysia ETF (EWM)

Financials: 43.8%

Industrials: 11.9%

Utilities: 11.5%

63.9%

These markets all have a much more diversified industry makeup and also trade at a substantial discount to other larger Asian stock markets. There are several key factors to note:

  • Political risks in Thailand have picked up in recent months, and this may not be the best market to hold in the coming months.
  • The Vaneck Indonesia ETF is a smaller ETF, but intriguing because it is not overly concentrated in financials, like its peer ETF, the iShares MSCI Indonesia ETF.
  • These economies, with the exception of Thailand, should grow by around 4-6% this year.
  • The top 10 holdings still account for over half of these exchange traded funds' assets.

The best approach to investing in Asia would be to invest in a basket of country ETFs, with a 5-20% weighting assigned to each one. These emerging markets, and even dominant markets like Singapore and Hong Kong, do not even account for 5% of the assets of many Asian ETFs. Furthermore, smaller, undervalued emerging markets like Indonesia, the Philippines, and Malaysia could be positioned to outperform China in the coming quarters.

data.title: TWOD: Baby Bond From Two Harbors Currently Overpriced
data.description:
Thesis

Two Harbors Investment Corp. CAL 30 (NYSE:TWOD) is a baby bond from the mortgage Reit ('mReit') Two Harbors (TWO). In today's article we are going to discuss the mReit's most recent financials as well as TWOD's structure and analytics, and highlight for investors why we believe the security is currently overpriced.

Q2 Results

The mReit just reported its latest financials, and the results are less than stellar:

The company recorded a large loss per share for the quarter, with a negative economic return on book value. Its leverage is still very high at 7x for its portfolio, which means negative returns are amplified.

While we are not investors in the common shares, it is important for a debt investor in the company to see a stable book value, or a slightly decreasing one. The trend here, however, is worrisome:

The book value had a massive hit, decreasing from $14.6 per share to $12.14 per share. The main driver for the book value decrease is a legacy lawsuit which has now been provisioned for:

On May 23, the court ruled that Two Harbors did not have grants to terminate its management agreement for costs. And so we, in consultation with our independent accountants and legal advisers, determined that the loss was not probable and estimable under ASC 450, which is the accounting standard that governs loss contingencies. The amount of the loss contingency accrual is the same $140 million that we initially reserved in 2020 related to the nonrenewal of the management agreement before reversing the accrual in connection with the subsequent termination for costs. The current accrual also includes an assumed statutory prejudgment interest at the simple rate of 9%. No other potential losses are probable or estimable at this time. We are waiting for a trial date to be set to resolve certain claims related to intellectual property and on the issues of potential damages for the contract termination.

The company stated that excluding the lawsuit accrual, the total quarterly economic return would have been -1.4%, a more palatable figure. While one-time losses are not necessarily worrisome when the underlying business is robust, it is not the case here. TWO is an mReit, not a company that makes widgets, thus any cash leaving the company is ultimately bad for bondholders.

Even without the reserve for the lawsuit contingency, the company would have lost money:

Please note the company lost $100 mm from its portfolio of agency bonds, MSR and hedges, versus a flat performance in Q1 2025. This simply means the managers did not do a good job in setting up the risk factors hedging correctly, and when you run 7x leverage this can hurt you.

As per its own disclosures the mReit will lose money irrespective of what Treasury rates do, and will only make money when mortgage spreads contract:

So if we look at the interest rate exposure we can see that when rates are up TWO loses value. When rates are down the mReit also loses value. The only positive scenario here is when mortgage spreads are down.

The company is set-up to produce positive net income when interest rates are not volatile (i.e. they do not move around) and when mortgage spreads are constant or contracting. In Q1 2025 that was the case, with positive net income for TWO coming from 'Net Interest and Servicing Income' at $133 mm. During that quarter the hedging was good, with the 'Mark-to-market' line item quasi-flat.

The take-away for an investor is that TWO is a volatile entity that can severely underperform due to its leverage during market turmoil. It does not care if risk free rates move up or down, because it loses money in both scenarios when markets are volatile and hedging is ineffective. TWO does best in a calm environment when it can just clip its interest and servicing income.

Baby bond

TWOD was issued in May 2025. The bond represents senior unsecured obligations for the company. Some of the characteristics and analytics for TWOD are:

  • Outstanding notional: $100 mm
  • Coupon: 9.375%
  • Par value: $25/share
  • Call date: 5/15/2027
  • Maturity date: 8/15/2030
  • Distribution dates: 2/15, 5/15, 8/15 & 11/15
  • Current yield: 9%
  • YTM: 8.4%

Since issuance the baby bonds have traded up, thus reducing the current yield and yield to maturity. Structurally speaking TWOD are a better risk/reward than the preferred shares, but like any financial product there is a price which is attractive for entry and a level which is stretched.

We find the current price stretched, especially in light of the limited upside here. While lower interest rates will help TWOD in time, a risk-off event can see the price move lower very fast, especially in light of the fundamental risk factors for the holding entity TWO which does very poorly when rates are volatile. We would love to buy TWOD at par or below, but see no upside at the current levels.

Retail investors, even buy and hold ones, need to understand that baby bonds do come down in price, and the best example that we have is the 2028 Carlyle Credit Income Fund NT 28 (NYSE:CCIA) which came down in price very fast from overstretched conditions at $26.6 per share:

You can read our 'Sell' article for that name here. The takeaway here is that an investor should not blindly buy a baby bond irrespective of the price, but wait for a good entry point. TWO is a volatile and leveraged entity that will spill over into a lower price for TWOD during times of turmoil. As we have seen with CCIA, overstretched prices do come down, and they can move lower quite fast.

We like TWOD structurally, but are of the opinion that the price is currently stretched to the upside.

Conclusion

TWOD is a bond that was issued in May 2025 and has traded up in price to $26/share with a current yield of 9%. TWO just reported its Q2 2025 financials that saw a massive hit to book value from a legacy lawsuit and a poor hedging performance during Q2 2025. While it is not time to panic, the results show how volatile this entity is, and cash leaving the company is bad for bondholders. Retail investors should not blindly buy a baby bond, but wait for a good entry point. We think the price action is currently stretched and would 'Sell' this name until a return to par.
